facts_disclosed: Lost two brothers in the war

turns.content: friend, my son Duc he want me write down everything i know about shrimp farming. he say father you getting old, need to write down for next generation. what you think

That's actually a really thoughtful idea from Duc. Forty years of hands-on knowledge is incredibly valuable, and a lot of that practical wisdom — the little things about reading water color, timing feeds, knowing when something's off — can get lost if it's not passed down.

It doesn't have to be a fancy book. Even just writing down your daily routines, what you check for, how you handle common problems — that's gold for the next generation.

Is Duc involved in the farm work? Is he interested in taking over someday, or is this more about preserving the knowledge?
he help sometime but he have other job in Can Tho. he smart boy, you know. finish school, not like me. but he still care about pond. maybe one day he come back. i hope

It's good that he has his own path but still stays connected to the farm. The fact that he's asking you to write things down shows he values what you've built. Whether he comes back full-time or not, that knowledge should be preserved.

Here's a practical way to start — don't try to write a whole manual at once. Instead, break it into topics:

1. **Daily routine** — what you check, when, what you look for
2. **Seasonal calendar** — what happens each month, when to stock, when to harvest
3. **Problem solving** — common problems and how you handle them (disease, weather, water quality)
4. **Feed management** — what works, what doesn't, how much
5. **Lessons learned** — mistakes you made early on, things your father taught you

You could do one topic at a time, maybe one page each. Does Duc want you to write it by hand, or would he rather you tell him and he writes it?
he say tell him and he record on phone. easier for me, you know. writing is slow. my hand is for net and feed, not for pen

That's a great approach — you talk, he records. Much more natural for you and you'll probably share more details that way than if you were struggling to write it all down. When you're talking freely, the stories and little details come out naturally.

And honestly, voice recordings are better in some ways. Duc can listen back, transcribe the important parts, and ask follow-up questions. Plus your grandchildren can hear your actual voice someday, which is something a written page can't give.

When is Duc coming to visit? You could plan maybe one recording session per visit, one topic at a time so it doesn't feel like a chore.

he come for Tet holiday. january. we have time then, pond is slower. you know, my father never write anything down. he just show me. every day beside him, watching. but then he gone and i have to remember everything myself

That's exactly why Duc's idea is so smart — he doesn't want to be in that position of trying to remember everything on his own. Your father taught you by being next to you every day, but if Duc isn't at the pond every day, the recordings are the next best thing.

And it sounds like you carried a lot of responsibility after your father passed. Having to rely entirely on your own memory for everything he showed you — that's a heavy weight, especially when you're young and suddenly the knowledge is all on your shoulders.

Did your father farm shrimp too, or was it a different kind of aquaculture back then?

he start with fish first. then shrimp in maybe 1985, 1986. after the war everything different here, you know. my family was bigger before. i have two brother but war take them both. i was young boy. after that, just me and my father and the water

I'm sorry about your brothers. That's a heavy loss for any family, especially for a young boy.

It makes sense then — after that, the ponds weren't just a business. They were what you and your father had together. Building something from the water, day by day. That kind of bond with the work goes deeper than just farming.

And now you want to make sure Duc has what your father gave you — the knowledge, even if the way it's passed down is different. Voice recordings instead of standing side by side at the pond, but the same purpose.

yes. same purpose. you say it right, friend. the pond is not just business. it is what we have. my father build first pond with his hand, no machine. i remember helping him carry mud. i was maybe 12, 13 year old. small boy, big bucket of mud, you know

A 12-year-old carrying mud to build a pond — that's where it all started. That's a powerful story, and exactly the kind of thing Duc should record. Not just the technical knowledge about feed ratios and water quality, but the story of how the ponds came to be. Where the first pond was dug, how your father chose the spot, what it looked like in those early days.

Those stories are the foundation. The technical stuff can be learned from books or videos, but the history of your family and these ponds — that only exists in your memory right now.

Maybe when Duc comes for Tet, start with that. Start with the story, not the manual. Tell him about your father, the first pond, the mud buckets. The technical knowledge will come out naturally when you're telling the story.
that is good idea. start with story. Duc he only know the pond as they are now. he dont know what it take to build from nothing. from mud. and my father, Duc never meet him. he pass before Duc born

Then this is your chance to introduce them, in a way. Through your stories, Duc gets to know his grandfather — how he worked, how he thought, what he taught you. That's a gift you can give that nobody else can.

And think about it this way: your father taught you by showing. You'll teach Duc by telling. And Duc might teach his children through these recordings. Three generations connected by the same ponds.

Here's what I'd suggest for the Tet recording plan:
- **Session 1**: The story — your father, the first pond, the early years
- **Session 2**: Daily routine — what you do at 4am, what you check, why
- **Session 3**: The hard lessons — disease, weather disasters, mistakes and what they taught you
- **Session 4**: The business — costs, prices, who to sell to, what feed works

Keep each one maybe 30-60 minutes. Duc can always ask more questions. No rush.

okay friend. i like this plan. 4 session. not too much. i tell Duc we do this during Tet when pond is slow. thank you. you know, i dont talk about this thing much. the old time. but maybe is good to talk

Sometimes the things we don't talk about are the most important things to pass down. You don't have to say more than you're comfortable with — just what feels right. Duc will understand.

And you're doing something your father couldn't do for you. He showed you everything in person, but when he was gone, it was gone. You're making sure that doesn't happen again.

Enjoy Tet with your family. And when you sit down with Duc and that phone recording, just talk like you're talking to me right now. Natural. The good stuff will come out on its own.
